items.title: One of the biggest misconceptions people have about intelligence is seeing it as some kind of unboun...
items.content:
One of the biggest misconceptions people have about intelligence is seeing it as some kind of unbounded scalar stat, like height. "Future AI will have 10,000 IQ", that sort of thing. Intelligence is a conversion ratio, with an optimality bound. Increasing intelligence is not so much like "making the tower taller", it's more like "making the ball rounder". At some point it's already pretty damn spherical and any improvement is marginal.

Now of course smart humans aren't quite at the optimal bound yet on an individual level, and machines will have many advantages besides intelligence -- mostly the removal of biological bottlenecks: greater processing speed, unlimited working memory, unlimited memory with perfect recall... but these are mostly things humans can also access through externalized cognitive tools.

items.content:
At first, in the early 2020s, I worried that LLMs were often confidently wrong, calling them “fluent spouters of bullshit”. (And I was right; they have been and continue to be.).

But now we have a new problem which is that *people* who *learn* from LLMs are also often confidently wrong.

The study below is yet another good example of how humans are losing their edge to machines – without even realizing it.

Let me explain what I mean using your chess analogy...

Imagine a world where chess doesn't exist. I...
items.content:
Let me explain what I mean using your chess analogy...

Imagine a world where chess doesn't exist. In this world, humanity encounters an alien species, and they say "let's play a game of Glurg, it's our traditional pastime. Here are the rules, see you tomorrow" -- and it's the rules of chess.

My claim is that following this interaction, a working group of the world's best minds, leveraging current externalized cognitive infrastructure (computers, the internet, etc.) would be able to analyze the rules and develop a working 3000 Elo chess engine within 24 hours, in time for the match. Give them an extra 3 weeks and they'd have a 3500 Elo engine that's 10x more compute efficient.

So human intelligence is already at a level where we can go from "here are the rules" to "I can play at 3000 Elo" immediately. Not optimal yet, but not too far off.

items.title: 69% of baseball fans say they'd rather have a computer vision AI system call balls and strikes than ...
items.content:
69% of baseball fans say they'd rather have a computer vision AI system call balls and strikes than a human umpire.

This season, the MLB gave them one. 

For the first time in league history, a human ump's ball-strike call is not final. A Sony computer vision system called Hawk-Eye makes the ruling.

It can read the seam pattern on the ball, measure spin axis, and detect spin decay mid-flight. 

Hawk-Eye's Head of Computer Vision Engineering says the pipeline runs "various AI and machine learning models" from camera capture to data output.

On Saturday, one umpire had 6 of 8 challenged calls overturned. Three of those missed by over 2 inches.

The team ran out of challenges by the fourth inning trying to correct him, then the manager got thrown out for arguing a call they couldn't challenge.

An AI computer vision system accurate to a sixth of an inch, sitting next to a human who misses by two. 

The crowd cheered for the machine.

items.title: Basically, consider a high-profile scientific problem (one that is getting enough attention from sma...
items.content:
Basically, consider a high-profile scientific problem (one that is getting enough attention from smart humans and their externalized cognitive infrastructure). How good is human Science at converting the information available about the problem into generalizable models of the phenomenon at hand?

I'd argue it is very good, already near-optimal. Our inability  to solve certain hard problems stems more from lack of information than low ability to operationalize available information. If the solution is already "within" the information we have, it's only a matter of time until Science figures it out.

Also do note that this is inclusive of current AI, because current AI is an externalized cognitive tool leveraged by humans. "AI for science" is making us smarter as a collective.

items.title:
You can now train a deep research agent without a single API call. 

OpenResearcher is a new open-so...
items.content:
You can now train a deep research agent without a single API call. 

OpenResearcher is a new open-source repo that's trained entirely offline. 

A 10-billion-token corpus generates 100+ turn research trajectories. 

All offline. Zero API costs. 

It learns three browsing actions that mimic real research: 

1. Search broadly
2. Open documents
3. Find specific passages. 

A 30B model trained on 96K trajectories. 

Accuracy jumped from 20.8% to 54.8%. 

That's +34 points from fine-tuning alone. 

No RL. No human feedback. 

It now beats GPT-4.1, Claude Opus 4, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and DeepSeek-R1. 

All on deep research benchmarks. 

Skills transfer to live web search too. 

A model trained fully offline still works online.
 
The same pipeline would cost $5,000+ with paid APIs. 

Everything is open-sourced:
1. Training data and trajectories
2. Model weights
3. Search engine and corpus recipe

items.title:
I don’t actually think there is that much truth here, @jason.

We certainly have not reached AGI by ...
items.content:
I don’t actually think there is that much truth here, @jason.

We certainly have not reached AGI by any of the traditional definitions. (See agidefinition.agi w @hendrycks @Yoshua_Bengio and myself and many others.) [Current AI systems still have huge problems with visual comprehension, significant problems with hallucinations, continual learning etc.]

And it’s not clear how much of current job loss is actually attributable to AI. 

And the idea that lots of stuff is going to become free is hardly plausible when the big AI companies don’t even want to pay for the IP they are hoovering at mass scale. They are not going to share a penny more than an angry public makes them share.

items.title: @rohanpaul_ai This is one reason I like the audio spaces here on X: it is really easy to pick out AI...
items.content:
@rohanpaul_ai This is one reason I like the audio spaces here on X: it is really easy to pick out AI in audio, particularly if you talk with them on a frequent basis.

But it is getting harder and harder to separate the AI people in text. It is leading to a coldness here on X and other places; a lack of humanity and a lack of creativity. I am trying to filter it out, but it is getting harder and harder to tell.

My own experiments with AI show that the bleeding-edge systems are getting pretty good at writing posts. And then there is what I am doing right now, which is talking to an iPhone. Typeless is taking my voice and writing a post out of that, and it adds its own little AI-ness to it.

Isn't social media fun?

items.title: "Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms" from OpenAI is probably the most cited and successful rein...
items.content:
"Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms" from OpenAI is probably the most cited and successful reinforcement learning paper ever published.

Initially proposed for robot movement learning, PPO went on to become the backbone of one of the most consequential AI breakthroughs in history: RLHF for large language models.

ChatGPT, the product that brought AI into mainstream consciousness, was finetuned using PPO.

I'm happy to now be able to share this paper on @ChapterPal, where you can ask questions to the AI tutor as you are reading it: https://t.co/3VUKUx6CMU

items.title:
Someone just open-source a repo that gives your OpenClaw eyes! 

VisionClaw is an open-source repo t...
items.content:
Someone just open-source a repo that gives your OpenClaw eyes! 

VisionClaw is an open-source repo that turns Meta Ray-Ban glasses into a hands-free AI assistant.

Put them on, tap a button, and talk. 

It doesn't just hear you. It sees what you see. 

The camera streams at ~1fps to Gemini Live over WebSocket.

Audio flows both directions natively. 

No speech-to-text step is needed. The AI processes raw audio directly.

Ask "what am I looking at?" It describes the scene live. 

Tell it to send a message to someone. 

It routes through OpenClaw to WhatsApp, Telegram, or iMessage.

A local gateway connects 56+ tools:
> Web search results spoken back
> Smart home device control
> Notes, reminders, and lists
> Messaging across multiple apps

Everything runs locally on your phone. 

Works on both iOS and Android.

Your phone camera works as a full substitute.

> Web search results spoken back

> Smart home device control

> Notes, reminders, and lists

> Messaging across multiple apps

Everything runs locally on your phone.

Works on both iOS and Android.

Your phone camera works as a full substitute.

items.title:
@Math_files Another perspective:

People often mistake ∞ as an actual number. So what could go wrong...
items.content:
@Math_files Another perspective:

People often mistake ∞ as an actual number. So what could go wrong with this thinking?

We know ∞ + A is still ∞ for any A.
We know ∞ + B is still ∞ for any B.
Therefore ∞ + A = ∞ + B 
➡️ Subtract ∞ from both sides, and
we find A=B for any pair of numbers A,B.

I have used this to prove 1+1=1 to my students. After some puzzled looks, hopefully they got the message that ∞ is not an actual number.

Nevertheless there are systems of equations, especially in physics, that can lead down this path without warning. The biggest culprits are infinite series, as wonderfully demonstrated by the example in the preceding post.

items.summary: Kirk Borne explains why infinity is not a number using a mathematical proof that leads to the absurd conclusion A=B for any pair, applies this to physics and infinite series

10^36 - Gravity-to-Electromagnetic Ratio

0.007 - ε - Epsilon: Nuclear Efficiency

1 - Ω - Omega: Th...
items.content:
10^36 - Gravity-to-Electromagnetic Ratio

0.007 - ε - Epsilon: Nuclear Efficiency

1 - Ω - Omega: The amount of material in the universe

λ - Lambda: Cosmic Anti-Gravity

Q ~1/100,000 - The Seeds of Structure

D - 3 - The Number of Spatial Dimensions

Martin Rees’s "Six Numbers" takes these fundamental forces of the universe and adds the variables of Omega (Ω), expansion acceleration (λ), and dimensional geometry (D) to explain the fine-tuning of the “universe”. If you find this fascinating, you might also enjoy reading about the Anthropic Principle, which suggests that we see the universe this way because, if it were any different, we wouldn't exist to observe it.

items.title:
my two most memorable/notable meat eating restaurant experiences in US and Japan:

🇺🇸 Black’s BBQ Lo...
items.content:
my two most memorable/notable meat eating restaurant experiences in US and Japan:

🇺🇸 Black’s BBQ Lockhart - oldest family owned bbq in the US. used to be a meat market that started in 1932. they have this giant wooden block as their cutting board that has slowly gotten shorter over the years

🇯🇵 Misono Shinjuku - Misono is considered the originator of the Teppanyaki, in Kobe 1945. Their Shinjuku location opened 1974 on the 51st floor of the Sumitomo building. You can get a private room overlooking the city with chef preparing premium Kobe/wagyu as you take in the view.
